ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, grilled, marinated — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for tandoor-grilled marinated chicken
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for chicken tikka specifically; A00LC (grilled chicken) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken tikka as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chicken tikka in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–6 boneless pieces of tandoor-grilled marinated chicken.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN (National Institute of Nutrition, India)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for tandoor-grilled chicken preparations. Values for chicken tikka are derived from the ICMR-NIN entry for tandoori chicken / grilled marinated chicken (boneless, skinless), which is the closest approved primary source match. The saffron and spice marinade contributes negligible macronutrient change. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted' (FDC ID 171477) to confirm plausibility of protein and fat ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database. Chicken tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dish (approximately 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). A GI of 30 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on: (1) grilled lean chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database which consistently show GI values in the 0–35 range for high-protein, very low-carbohydrate meat dishes; (2) the principle that foods with negligible available carbohydrate have a low GI by definition. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and cooking process
hypertension.guidance: Chicken tikka contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (640 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents about 28% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt marinades where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.5 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30, chicken tikka produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and is a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is also monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein source with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken tikka provides lean grilled protein with approximately 1.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the tandoor-grilling method avoids added cooking oils, and the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) may confer modest cardioprotective benefit, though sodium content warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken tikka is a classic North Indian dish originating from the Punjab region, consisting of boneless chicken pieces marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture and grilled at high temperature in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The marinade typically includes yogurt, saffron, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and oil, which tenderise the meat and impart a characteristic smoky, charred flavour with vibrant orange-red colour. The resulting dish is juicy, slightly charred on the exterior, and tender within, with a complex spiced aroma. In Singapore, chicken tikka is widely served at North Indian restaurants as a starter or main course, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, chicken tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 25 g protein and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-glycemic and high-protein dietary patterns. The glycemic index is low (estimated ~30), and the glycemic load per standard serving is negligible at approximately 1.5. Saturated fat is moderate at 1.8 g per 100 g. Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) is the primary clinical consideration for hypertensive patients. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified chicken. It is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Clinically, chicken tikka is a favourable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, provided portion size and sodium intake are monitored.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken tikka?
Is chicken tikka good for diabetics?
Is chicken tikka high in protein?
What is the GI of chicken tikka?
How is chicken tikka different from tandoori chicken?
What spices are in chicken tikka?
Can I eat chicken tikka on a low carb diet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for tandoor-grilled marinated chicken (the closest approved primary source for this Indian dish) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for plausibility. No single ICMR-NIN entry is titled exactly 'chicken tikka with saffron'; the adaptation is defensible but not an exact match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (tandoor-grilled chicken / tandoori chicken entry) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (chicken breast, roasted). The saffron and spice marinade contributes negligible macronutrient change to the base values. 2. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 3. Sodium value (320 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with spiced marinade; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 4. The food_name has been normalised to 'Chicken Tikka' (canonical English form) from the input 'Chicken Tikka (Chef Recommendation)'; the 'Chef Recommendation' qualifier is a menu-marketing label and is not part of the canonical food identity. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible parent category for grilled chicken; a dedicated chicken tikka code does not exist in FoodEx2 as of this processing date. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for lean grilled chicken per USDA and ICMR-NIN data.
